title: SECURE Act 2.0 and Legislative Updates
description: Apply key provisions of SECURE Act 2.0
topics: Apply key provisions of SECURE Act 2.0
Explain changes to RMD rules and age thresholds
Evaluate new Roth options in employer plans
Analyze emergency savings provisions

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: SECURE 2.0 (2022) builds on the original SECURE Act (2019), implementing dozens of retirement planning changes. Understanding these provisions is essential for current practice.

sections.title: RMD Penalty Reduction
sections.content: Old rule: 50% penalty on missed RMD. New rule (SECURE 2.0): 25% penalty, reduced to 10% if corrected within 2 years.

sections.title: Roth Accounts in Plans
sections.content: Starting 2024, Roth 401(k)/403(b) accounts have NO RMDs for original owner. This aligns them with Roth IRAs—major planning change.

sections.title: Mandatory Roth Catch-Ups
sections.content: Starting 2026, catch-up contributions for those earning >$145,000 must be designated Roth. No more pre-tax catch-ups for high earners.

sections.title: New Account Types and Features
rows: 529 must be open 15+ years, $35,000 lifetime limit per beneficiary
Simplified plan with IRA limits, exempt from testing
Employer can match student loan payments

sections.title: Auto-Enrollment Expansion
sections.content: Effective 2025, new 401(k) and 403(b) plans must include automatic enrollment: Initial rate 3-10%, auto-escalation 1%/year until 10-15%, opt-out permitted. Exempted: Plans before 2025, employers with <10 employees, churches, governmental plans.

sections.title: Summary
items: RMD age rises to 73 (2023) and 75 (2033)
No RMDs from Roth 401(k)/403(b) starting 2024
Super catch-up for ages 60-63 starting 2025
529-to-Roth transfers now possible with restrictions
Student loan matching helps debt-burdened savers

knowledgeCheck.question: Under SECURE Act 2.0, what is the standard penalty for failing to take a Required Minimum Distribution (RMD) if the failure is NOT corrected within two years?
options: There is no penalty under SECURE Act 2.0
knowledgeCheck.explanation: SECURE Act 2.0 reduced the penalty for failing to take an RMD to 25%. The penalty can be further reduced to 10% if the error is corrected within two years. If not corrected within two years, the penalty remains at 25%.

reveal.question: Under SECURE Act 2.0, what's a key benefit for lower-income workers regarding employer-sponsored emergency savings accounts?
reveal.answer: Employers may be able to automatically enroll employees in emergency savings accounts, often Roth-designated, and may be able to match employee contributions, providing a significant boost to savings for those who need it most.

sections.title: RMD Penalty: Before and After SECURE Act 2.0

rows.label: Reduced Penalty if Corrected Within 2 Years

title: Tax-Efficient Retirement Income Strategies
description: Optimize withdrawal sequencing across account types
topics: Optimize withdrawal sequencing across account types
Apply tax bracket management strategies
Evaluate Roth conversion opportunities in retirement
Minimize impact on Social Security and Medicare

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: How you withdraw from retirement accounts matters as much as how you save. Strategic withdrawal sequencing can save hundreds of thousands in lifetime taxes.

sections.title: Why Sequencing Matters
sections.content: Withdrawing from the wrong account can push you into higher tax brackets, increase Medicare premiums (IRMAA), increase Social Security taxation, and trigger Net Investment Income Tax.

sections.title: Dynamic Withdrawal Strategy
rows: Satisfy RMDs, minimize other taxable income
Tap Roth accounts to avoid bracket creep

sections.title: Calculating Conversion "Room"
sections.content: Example: Standard deduction (MFJ) $30,700, Other income $30,000, Top of 12% bracket $99,400. "Room" in 12% bracket = $99,400 - $30,700 - $30,000 = $38,700. Convert up to $38,700 without entering 22% bracket.

sections.title: Key Strategy
sections.content: Roth withdrawals don't count in "combined income" for SS taxation or IRMAA thresholds. Use Roth to keep combined income below critical thresholds.

sections.title: IRMAA Planning
sections.content: Medicare premiums increase for higher earners based on MAGI from 2 years prior. Strategy: Manage income two years before Medicare enrollment, do large Roth conversions before age 63 (before affecting premiums at 65), use Roth distributions to stay below thresholds.

sections.title: Summary
items: Sequence withdrawals to minimize lifetime taxes, not just this year's
Fill lower brackets with taxable income; use Roth for overflow
Convert to Roth during low-income years before RMDs and SS
Roth withdrawals don't affect SS taxation or IRMAA
Plan two years ahead for Medicare premium management

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following strategies is MOST effective for managing tax brackets during retirement withdrawals?
options: Maximizing withdrawals from tax-deferred accounts early in retirement, regardless of current tax bracket.
Withdrawing from Roth accounts exclusively to avoid any current taxation.
Strategically combining withdrawals from taxable, tax-deferred, and tax-free accounts to stay within desired tax brackets.
Ignoring tax implications and focusing solely on maximizing investment returns.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Strategic tax bracket management involves carefully planning withdrawals from different account types to avoid pushing into higher tax brackets. This often means combining taxable, tax-deferred, and tax-free withdrawals to optimize the tax burden.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the impact of different account types on your taxable income.

reveal.question: What does IRMAA stand for, and why is it important to consider during retirement income planning?
reveal.answer: IRMAA stands for Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount. It's important because it can significantly increase Medicare Part B and Part D premiums for high-income retirees. Strategic withdrawal planning can help minimize or avoid IRMAA surcharges.

headers: Pros of Roth Conversions in Retirement
Cons of Roth Conversions in Retirement

rows.label: Tax Implications
values: Pay taxes now at potentially lower rates than future required minimum distributions (RMDs).
Conversion amount is taxed as ordinary income in the year of conversion.

rows.label: Future Tax Benefits
values: Qualified withdrawals in retirement are tax-free, providing tax diversification.
Can increase current tax liability and potentially push you into a higher tax bracket or trigger IRMAA.

rows.label: Estate Planning
values: May not be beneficial if you anticipate being in a lower tax bracket in retirement.

title: Early Retirement Strategies
description: Apply Rule of 55 and SEPP (72(t)) exceptions
topics: Apply Rule of 55 and SEPP (72(t)) exceptions
Evaluate bridge strategies to age 59½
Calculate sustainable withdrawal rates for early retirees
Plan healthcare coverage before Medicare eligibility

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Retiring before 59½ requires careful planning to access retirement funds without penalties. Understanding the available exceptions is essential.

sections.title: Rule of 55
sections.content: Penalty-free access to 401(k)/403(b) if you leave employment at age 55 or older in the year of separation. Only applies to the plan at that employer—not IRAs, not rolled-over plans from previous employers. SECURE 2.0: Lowered to age 50 for public safety employees.

sections.title: Exam Tip
sections.content: If you quit at 54, even if you turn 55 the same year, the Rule of 55 doesn't apply. You must be 55 or older in the year of separation.

sections.title: SEPP (72(t)) Requirements
rows: 5 years or until age 59½, whichever is LATER

sections.title: SEPP Calculation Example
sections.content: IRA Balance: $1,000,000, Age: 50. Life Expectancy Method: Balance / 34.2 years = ~$29,240/year. Fixed Amortization (at 4%): ~$52,000/year. Warning: Locked in for 9.5 years (longer of 5 years or until 59½). Cannot modify without retroactive penalty.

sections.title: Taxable Account Bridge Strategy
sections.content: Use taxable investments to fund living expenses until age 59½, leaving retirement accounts intact. Ages 50-54: Live on taxable account. Age 55: Rule of 55 unlocks 401(k). Age 59½: All accounts accessible. Tax efficiency: Long-term capital gains may be taxed at 0%/15%/20%.

sections.title: Roth Conversion Ladder
items: 1. Convert Traditional IRA to Roth each year
2. Wait 5 years (seasoning requirement)
3. Withdraw converted amounts penalty-free
Each conversion has its own 5-year clock
Requires planning 5+ years ahead

sections.title: ACA Subsidy Strategy
sections.content: Keep MAGI below 400% of FPL for premium tax credits (~$60,240 single, ~$124,800 family of 4). Use Roth withdrawals (not counted in MAGI) to stay below subsidy thresholds.

sections.title: Summary
items: Rule of 55 unlocks 401(k) at separation from service at 55+
SEPP/72(t) allows any-age access with serious commitment
Taxable accounts bridge to 59½ at favorable rates
Roth ladder provides penalty-free access after 5-year seasoning
Healthcare costs are the biggest early retirement wildcard

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, age 56, is considering early retirement. She currently works for Acme Corp and has a 401(k) with them. She also has a traditional IRA funded with rollovers from previous employers. If Sarah separates from service with Acme Corp this year, which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding penalty-free withdrawals?
options: Sarah can take penalty-free withdrawals from both her Acme Corp 401(k) and her traditional IRA.
Sarah can take penalty-free withdrawals only from her traditional IRA.
Sarah can take penalty-free withdrawals only from her Acme Corp 401(k).
Sarah must wait until age 59½ to take penalty-free withdrawals from both her Acme Corp 401(k) and her traditional IRA.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Rule of 55 allows penalty-free withdrawals from a 401(k) or 403(b) plan if the employee separates from service in or after the year they turn age 55. However, this rule only applies to the plan at the *current* employer where the separation occurs. Rolled-over IRAs are not eligible for the Rule of 55 exception.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which accounts are directly tied to her current employer.

reveal.question: How long must SEPP (72(t)) payments continue to avoid a retroactive penalty?
reveal.answer: Payments must continue for at least 5 years, or until age 59½, whichever period is longer. Modifying or stopping payments before this requirement is met results in a retroactive penalty on all prior distributions.

title: Working in Retirement
description: Apply Social Security earnings test rules
topics: Apply Social Security earnings test rules
Analyze impact of retirement work on benefits
Evaluate encore career and phased retirement options
Plan for continued retirement contributions

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Many retirees continue working for income, purpose, or both. Understanding how work affects retirement benefits is essential.

sections.title: Good News
sections.content: Withheld benefits aren't lost forever. At FRA, benefits are recalculated to account for withheld months, resulting in higher monthly benefit. May "break even" within 12-15 years.

sections.title: Delayed Retirement Credits
sections.content: Each year past FRA: 8% increase until age 70. Working past FRA and delaying benefits provides both delayed retirement credits AND potentially higher AIME from continued earnings.

sections.title: HSA Considerations
sections.content: Enrolled in Medicare Part A or B: Cannot contribute to HSA. Still on HDHP, not on Medicare: Can contribute. Stop HSA contributions 6 months before Medicare (retroactive Part A enrollment).

sections.title: Phased Retirement Options
items: Employer programs: Reduced hours, in-service distributions (62+), mentoring role
Self-directed: Contract/consultant, encore career, part-time W-2

sections.title: Summary
items: Earnings test reduces SS benefits before FRA (but increases later)
Investment income doesn't count against earnings test
Delayed retirement credits (8%/year) reward working past FRA
Medicare enrollment timing matters when employer coverage exists
Stop HSA contributions 6 months before Medicare enrollment

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, age 63, is considering taking Social Security benefits while continuing to work. In 2024, she expects to earn $60,000. Assuming the annual earnings limit for those under FRA is $22,320, what is the approximate reduction in her Social Security benefits for the year?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: For every $2 earned above the annual limit ($22,320), Social Security benefits are reduced by $1. Sarah's earnings exceed the limit by $60,000 - $22,320 = $37,680. Dividing this amount by 2 gives the reduction in benefits: $37,680 / 2 = $18,840. Because her benefits are reduced by $1 for every $2 earned, and we want to calculate the reduction in benefits, we take the amount OVER the limit and divide by 2. This result is incorrect because it's asking for her benefits reduction, not how much she can earn before reduction. The reduction is $1 for every $2 earned over the limit, so you divide the excess amount by 2 to get the benefit reduction. Therefore, ($60,000 - $22,320) / 2 = $18,840. However, the reduction is only applied to the amount of benefits she receives, so we need to determine if she will receive that amount in benefits for the year. It's possible that her benefits would be suspended entirely. This question is designed to test comprehension of the earnings test rules.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the $2 for $1 reduction rule for those under Full Retirement Age (FRA).

reveal.question: What is the annual percentage increase in Social Security benefits for each year benefits are delayed past Full Retirement Age (FRA)?

rows.label: Definition
values: A new career pursued after a traditional retirement age, often in a different field.
A gradual reduction in work hours or responsibilities leading up to full retirement, often with the same employer.

rows.label: Employer
values: Typically a new employer or self-employment.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Often driven by a desire for purpose, social impact, or continued income.
Primarily focused on a smooth transition to full retirement.

title: Annuities in Retirement Income Planning
description: Compare immediate vs. deferred annuity structures
topics: Compare immediate vs. deferred annuity structures
Evaluate income annuity features and guarantees
Apply annuity taxation rules
Determine appropriate annuity allocation in retirement

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Annuities can provide guaranteed lifetime income. Understanding when and how to use them helps create secure retirement income plans.

sections.title: SPIA - How It Works
sections.content: Pay lump sum to insurance company. Begin receiving lifetime monthly payments immediately. Payments continue until death (or term/period certain).

sections.title: SPIA Payment Options
rows: Covers two lives; lower initial payout

sections.title: Deferred Income Annuity (DIA/QLAC)
sections.content: Pay premium now, receive income starting in the future (often 10-20 years later). Benefits: Longevity insurance, lower cost (mortality credits), RMD reduction with QLAC.

sections.title: QLAC Rules
rows: QLAC value excluded from RMD calculation
Can be as late as age 85

sections.title: Variable Annuity with GLWB
sections.content: Guaranteed Lifetime Withdrawal Benefit: Benefit base (notional value) for calculating withdrawals. Withdrawal rate typically 4-6% of benefit base guaranteed for life. Step-ups if market performs well. Fees: 0.75-1.5% for rider plus fund expenses.

sections.title: Exclusion Ratio Example
sections.content: Premium $100,000, Expected return (total payments) $200,000. Exclusion ratio = 50%. Monthly payment $1,000: Taxable $500, Tax-free return of principal $500.

sections.title: Summary
items: SPIAs provide immediate guaranteed income for life
DIAs/QLACs provide longevity insurance starting later
Variable annuities with GLWB offer market upside with income floor
Exclusion ratio determines tax treatment of non-qualified annuities
Annuitize essential expenses—don't over-commit liquidity

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements BEST describes the primary difference between a Single Premium Immediate Annuity (SPIA) and a Deferred Income Annuity (DIA)?
options: A SPIA begins payments immediately upon purchase, while a DIA delays payments to a future date.
A SPIA is always tax-qualified, while a DIA is always non-qualified.
A SPIA offers a death benefit, while a DIA does not.
A SPIA is only available for individuals over age 70, while a DIA is available at any age.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The key difference lies in the timing of payments. SPIAs provide immediate income, whereas DIAs defer payments to a later date. The other options are not universally true.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider when the income stream begins for each type of annuity.

reveal.question: How can a Qualified Longevity Annuity Contract (QLAC) help reduce Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs)?
reveal.answer: A QLAC allows a portion of retirement assets to be excluded from the RMD calculation until payments begin. This is because the assets used to purchase the QLAC are not considered part of the account balance subject to RMDs, potentially lowering the annual RMD amount.

sections.title: SPIA vs. DIA: A Closer Look

rows.label: Cost
values: Generally Lower (due to mortality credits and time value of money)

rows.label: Suitability
values: Younger individuals planning for very late-life income needs
